title: Cooper quartets and fractional vortices in frustrated Josephson junction dice arrays
authors: Erik Lennart Weerda, Olav F. Syljuåsen, Matteo Rizzi, Michele Burrello
abstract: Superconductivity mediated by Cooper quartets of charge 4e is a phenomenon of key importance for both the understanding of certain exotic superconductors and the engineering of quantum memories protected at the hardware level by topological order. Josephson junction arrays in the shape of the dice lattice constitute one of the main candidates for the realization of this phase of matter. Here, we analyze numerical signatures of the emergence of this exotic phase when superconducting dice arrays are frustrated by inserting one third of a flux quantum per rhombic plaquette. We adopt simulations of relaxation dynamics to study the critical current of such devices and analyse the Fourier decomposition of their bulk supercurrents. A further characterization of these systems at finite temperature is obtained through Monte Carlo techniques and two-dimensional infinite tensor networks. Our results indicate that the observed peaks of the critical current and temperature at frustration 1/3 correspond to a superconductor-insulator phase transition compatible with the deconfinement of half-vortices, while the low-temperature correlation functions of the model confirm the onset of a 4e superconducting phase mediated by Cooper quartets. We finally address the effects of Josephson energy and flux disorder typical of experimental arrays, and comment on the role of charging energies in the corresponding two-dimensional quantum model.
summary: This paper analyzes superconducting behavior in frustrated Josephson junction dice arrays, predicting that the system enters a $4e$ superconducting phase when frustrated at $f=1/3$. By using advanced numerical and theoretical methods, the authors confirm this $4e$ state through the dominance of the second supercurrent harmonic and the algebraic decay of the corresponding correlation function.
main_problem: The study investigates the emergence of exotic superconducting phases mediated by Cooper quartets ($4e$) in frustrated Josephson junction (JJ) dice arrays.
main_result: The critical current peaks at a frustration of $f=1/3$ are linked to a superconductor-insulator transition compatible with half-vortex deconfinement, confirming the onset of a $4e$ superconducting phase.
method: The research employs a combination of numerical simulations (relaxation dynamics), Monte Carlo techniques, and advanced theoretical tools like infinite tensor networks and Renormalization Group (RG) analysis.
model_or_system: The system is a Josephson junction array (JJA) on a dice lattice, modeled by an XY Hamiltonian. The analysis focuses on the effect of magnetic frustration ($f$) and the competition between $2e$ (Cooper pair) and $4e$ (Cooper quartet) superconducting correlations.
key_observables: Critical current ($I_c$), critical temperature ($T_c$), supercurrent harmonics ($I_1$ vs $I_2$), and correlation functions ($	ilde{C}_{2e}(r)$ vs $	ilde{C}_{4e}(r)$).
important_parameters: Magnetic frustration ($f$), charging energy ($E_C$), Josephson energy ($E_J$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes negligible charging energy for the classical XY model description, though later sections incorporate $E_C$ effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dice lattice geometry, the dominance of the $I_2$ (quartet) component at $f=1/3$, and the algebraic decay of the $4e$ correlation function ($	ilde{C}_{4e}(r)$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical model and analyzing the critical current dependence on frustration ($f$), to characterizing the supercurrent harmonics ($I_1$ vs $I_2$), and finally delving into the quantum nature using correlation functions and RG analysis to confirm the $4e$ phase.
why_interesting: The work provides a concrete, tunable platform (JJ arrays) to study fractional charge carriers and topological order, bridging concepts from superconductivity, frustrated magnetism, and quantum phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of a JJA in dice lattice geometry. The red arrows show the unit-cell vectors. By applying a perpendicular magnetic field to the array, a magnetic flux Φ can be threaded through every plaquette.

Figure 2. Critical currents as a function of frustration f for a 50 × 50 dice lattice calculated using the phase-bias relaxation method. The inset shows corresponding results for a 100×100 square lattice. The critical currents are normalized by Ic(0). Each purple dot shows the critical current obtained from a single phase configuration. A lower bound on the true critical current can be estimated by taking the maximum of these values (black line). The fringes at low values of f are finite- size effects.

Figure 3. Energy per island (colored curve) and its derivative (grey) vs. f for a 50×50 dice lattice without source and drain islands, i.e. free boundary conditions, obtained by simulated annealing and a subsequent relaxation step, see App. A.

Figure 4. Typical current-phase relation for f = 1/3 for a phase-biased supercurrent between source (purple) and drain (pink) islands situated in the middle of a 50 × 50 dice lattice. I is expressed in units of 2e

Figure 5. Harmonics of the supercurrent across a single rhom- bus as a function of f. The source and drain islands for this supercurrent are highlighted in purple (source) and pink (drain) in the inset. These islands are situated in the middle of a 50 × 50 dice lattice, and the supercurrent is determined by the introduction of a phase bias between them, see App. A. Points and error bars indicate the mean and standard error of the mean from 100 independent simulations for each value of f.
